pros: *Incentives if you know how to use them, FREE flights (standby) and ZED (standby for other airlines or small fee) *Always giving merch and free massages on health days and snacks and free coffee! *PTO (personal time off) granted daily to get off early without pay if you want to take it. cons: *Youre stuck with the hours they give you (you bid on shifts and seniority gets the best ones) *started at LOW pay BUT within a year got 4 bonuses *very clique-ish with management *STATS are rediculous to keep up with. 40+ calls a day. * You swap managers and teams every few weeks so just when you build rapport, youre starting over again. *Job stated WFH but I never worked from home-had to be in office everyday. *High turnover because of the stats and hours and I guess starting pay

title: The Proof is in the Pudding
body: The Proof is in the Pudding
pros: It's a great company to work for. You'll here people complain about naturally, or how great something are at other companies, but they wont leave because for the most part, its still better here. We can Definitely improve on things YES, however, there is not perfect company and never will be. This position is one of those of seniority and it really does get better every year, even if you have so much more control of your schedule than other airlines. You get to meet some amazing co workers that inspire you, and you'll most likely make fast friends with coworkers. You get to experience little bits of the world, and collect a great wardrobe. Just remember to keep positive and keep kindness. Flight Attendants complain plenty, its easy to forget your blessings and fall into the negative pot.
cons: It can be hard to find the work life balance. Between eating well, sleeping schedules, finding a place to live if you moved, commuting if you don't move, cash pads, dealing with very different personalities (especially flight attendants) family time, not gaining 15 pounds, and plenty more- it can take years to find your balance.

title: Delta Ramp Agent
pros: Being a ramp agent comes with several advantages that make the role appealing to many. One of the biggest pros is the active, fast-paced environmentârather than sitting at a desk, ramp agents are constantly moving, loading baggage, guiding aircraft, and operating equipment, which keeps the job physically engaging. The role also offers strong teamwork experiences, as ramp agents rely on coordination and communication to keep flights running smoothly and on time. Many find satisfaction in knowing they play a crucial part in the aviation industry, directly impacting safety and efficiency. Additionally, the position can come with great travel benefits, such as discounted or free flights, which allow ramp agents and their families to explore new destinations. Finally, it can serve as a stepping stone into a long-term career in aviation, with opportunities to advance into supervisory, maintenance, or operations roles.
cons: Being a ramp agent also comes with challenges that may be seen as cons. The work is very physically demanding, involving heavy lifting, bending, and working outdoors in all types of weatherâwhether itâs freezing cold, pouring rain, or extreme heat. Shifts are often irregular, with early mornings, late nights, weekends, and holidays being the norm since airports run around the clock. The noise from jet engines and constant movement of heavy machinery can make the environment stressful and require strict attention to safety protocols. Additionally, the pace is fast and time-sensitive, which can create pressure when delays or issues arise. While travel perks are a big benefit, the starting pay for ramp agents is often modest compared to the intensity of the work. Overall, it can be a tough job physically and mentally, requiring resilience and adaptability.

title: Delta Air Lines Sales Associate
pros: While flight benefits are a valued perk, their accessibility can be challenging for employees without long-standing tenure, which may impact their ability to fully utilize this offering. Additionally, while unionized workforces often enjoy comprehensive benefits packages, Deltaâs prohibition of unions limits employeesâ ability to advocate collectively for improvements or expanded benefits.
cons: While leadership emphasizes valuing employees and promoting principles like humility and servant leadership, there seems to be a disconnect between these values and the reality many employees experience. For instance, micromanagement and limited autonomy in decision-making have impacted morale and efficiency, making it challenging for employees to perform at their best. Additionally, the organizational culture sometimes feels more political than merit-based. Advancement opportunities often appear to be influenced by relationships and DE&I rather than individual contributions or performance. This can create barriers for employees striving to grow through their hard work and skillset. Iâve also observed that the HR function seems primarily aligned with managerial interests, which has led to concerns about the support and receptiveness offered to employees. Addressing concerns with HR can sometimes result in negative repercussions, which discourages open and honest communication.

title: Great pay and flexible job!
pros: The flexiblity in your schedule is AMAZING! I've received two rasies in less than two years PLUS profit sharing. It's incredible and I've never worked at a company so committed to hearing our voices. You can make as much as you want if you are willing to work and the money stays in your pocket because the flight attendants at Delta are not unionized. The flexibility in your schedule is AMAZING. The company even offers leaves of absence in slower months, which allow you to take a break and pursue other interests.
cons: The training and first two years are a beast and require that you really commit to the job and company financially. The career is very expensive to enter but well worth your time, sacrifice, and endurance.

title: Great benefits, Disappointing management
pros: The benefits are pretty good, including free travel, medical, vision, and dental coverage, profit sharing, and yearly raises.
cons: Management frequently shows favoritism based on personal relationships. Despite raising concerns with HR, no meaningful action has been taken. Itâs clear that HR prioritizes protecting the company rather than addressing legitimate issues. The environment at the Dallas office feels disconnected from the Delta experience seen in other locations, which creates inconsistency and frustration for employees. Micromanagement is currently at an all time high. While leads are given the ability to provide rewards to employees one on one meetings typically focus on negative feedback.

title: An excellent entry-level, well-paid customer service position
body: An excellent entry-level, well-paid customer service position
pros: Great work from home opportunities and highly competitive pay for the position. Rarely ever asked to work outside of standard hours. Day-to-day, you know what to expect.
cons: High call volumes. Airline passengers are more of an upmarket clientele on average, so negative attitudes being expressed by callers are not necessarily as crude as in other customer service positions. However, this segment of the market is far more likely to be upset by minor inconveniences, so it's important to expect a lot of unhappy people (who generally express their unhappiness very politely, but insist upon getting their way).

title: A Major Airline That Requires Major Changes
body: A Major Airline That Requires Major Changes
pros: Delta is the first company to pay their flight attendants for boarding an aircraft! Although working for Delta Air lines has its benefits many changes are needed in order to make a fair livable wage for new hire and senior flight attendants. Flight attendants face many sacrifices and daily exhaustion. Such as time away from family and a rapid decline in health in order to have an inflight career. One would say that the benefits do not outweigh the drawbacks and these disadvantages. Many are also grateful for the benefits and being able to better connect the world. Ultimately, this line of work isn't for everyone. Delta can make it easy in some ways and not in others.
cons: You don't always get the schedule you want. Once you learn how to make your scheduling preferences work for you and your seniority your schedule will get better with time.

title: Good money, but thatâs not everything
body: Good money, but thatâs not everything
pros: Profit sharing was around 10% or higher and people are generally friendly, flight benefits are nice to have. Seriously, they are very nice and friendly people to be around. One of the most positive working environments on a surface level. A lot of mandatory corporate surveys that tell you the guys upstairs are listening (even if they claim to be confidential). Delta care and giving back to the community is super positive and there are a lot of opportunities to do superficial things to make yourself feel like a better person.
cons: Architecture for any kind of data analysis, science, engineering is seriously lacking. Documentation is not a thing. There is a serious international conflict between legacy leadership who want to divest from data and newer management who want to invest in data. The data infrastructure is very poor, constantly changing, and always seems cobbled together with bandaids. Servers are frequently crashing.

title: Working at Delta
pros: Flight benefits. Company culture. Volunteer opportunities. EAP.
cons: Sedgwick processes (it practically twists your arm to try and go on any sort of leave). Health insurance could be better. Strict attendance policies- when combined with the difficulty to go on a leave approved by Sedgwick, if you have any sort of disability or medical condition, it makes life Hell on Earth. No overtime paid unless approved by manager or posted- even if youâre picking up hours from coworkers and working up to 80-90 hours a week, youâre being paid the base rate. Not as much support on tough calls. Collaboration with support teams on calls often leads to even more stress due to supervisors sometimes being short tempered. They do not seem to be held accountable as much as the lower ranking employees are and often get away with being downright rude or unhelpful.

People here live by a different set of rules. If you have a reputable degree, are a smart individual, know basic math, and you are younger than 50, run! I would strongly advise against a job at TechOps, youâll be extremely unhappy. Outside of a select few, management here is really bad, no leadership skills, no strategic vision, terrible communication and illogical expectations. Select few also act like children. What ends up happening is promotions have to do very little with actual capabilities but rather how well you are liked by the hiring manager. When this process happens repeatedly you end up with the majority of leaders being incapable. TechOps has reached this point. I think Finance is the only department at TechOps who doesnât have this problem but their management is all newly fresh.
